my_thoughts: The sigh + shift + window look is pure passive withdrawal. 'Yes' admits teacher is right but body says 'I'll do it anyway.' This is the pattern: acknowledge verbally, continue behaviorally. Teacher's relentless observation ('Do you notice how you look out the window...') doesn't let them off the hook. Each turn: avoid gaze, avoid eyes, avoid by looking out window. The tracking is systematic. But student isn't examining WHY, just admitting THAT. The sarcasm coming ('I like the view') will be the breaking point—can't stay passive forever under observation pressure.
accumulated_insight: T3: Student withdraws nonverbally: '[Sighs, shifts in his chair.] Yes. [Looks out the window.]'—passive withdrawal replaces verbal defense. The 'Yes' acknowledges mirroring but body language (sigh, shift, window) shows continued avoidance. From justification (T2) to passive withdrawal (T3): verbal rationalizations exhausted, now just DOING the avoidance openly. Teacher relentlessly tracks behavior ('you look out the window when you talk to me'). UM_04 pattern: stay with behavioral data. Predicting no_clear_effect—student is stuck in pattern, not examining it.
visible_response: Do you notice how you look out the window when you talk to me?
